speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 6. Today is October 23, 1996. And we're interviewing Estelle Laughlin. I had asked you about your first impressions of Majdanek, what you saw, what you heard, what you smelled. What can you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I paused at--

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about children.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes, how we were dumped on the fields, and how, how thirsty and hungry we were, and how they hosed down the fields for us to lick the water. And then they-- all the men and women and children were sorted out. The children-- and of course, I thought-- and I mean, as, as, as, as ridiculous it seems because I don't believe that on a conscious level, I expected to survive. But nevertheless, being separated from my, my family was very, very frightening. And I-- being that young, I felt even more threatened. And, you know, going into the field with the other children was very frightening. Now, the men were separated. And I was not separated from the women. I remained with the women. And the men-- we were separated from the men. And, at that point, it was sorting out where the men were sitting, standing, gathered, on one half of one part of the field. And we were on the other part of the field. Now, all of us had cyanide sewn into our lapels. And that was as a last resort. My father was already then ill with TB. And he was sitting across from us. He was very chilled. We were very chilled. And he had, like, a turban, something. He was so skinny by then. And he had that-- like a turban, something, to wrap around his head because he was so chilled. I remember I ran. I dared, because the Germans were on the side-- that I dared to run over to my father and tell him, Father, don't worry. I was having good news for him. Don't worry. They are not-- they won't kill me. I know I have cyanide. Remember, Dad, I have cyanide. They won't kill me. [CRYING] And my father said, child, [SOBBING] don't do it. [CRYING] Maybe that's how-- maybe that was a spark of will that he gave me. But he also gave me that cyanide. But things was so terrible that my, my, my relief at the moment was-- that I have the escape because I'm going to poison myself. And they won't get me. Well, my father was-- that was the last we saw of our father. The next time that we had any any sort of communication about our father was quite a bit later. When we were taken in behind the barbed wires, and we were in the barracks, and we were sent to do forced labor-- that my sister once was marching with a-- with a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she saw from the distance, not too far passing, a, a, a, a group of men. And she recognized something-- someone. And she said, oh, where is my father? And he said, look, see that wagon over there, the truck. There was a truck smoldering with bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, your father is there. And that was the last we-- or, you know, the last communication. Well, we knew that our father would be the least likely to survive because he was already so-- in such poor physical health. But nevertheless, [LAUGHS] my sister was still hoping. After, after coming back to the field and coming back to where my father was separated with a man on that field across from us, the next thing I remember is taking through the showers. Well, that, too, was a petrifying experience, because we knew of the showers. And we knew that, in most cases, what came out of the shower was, was the gas. So we expected to be gassed. And-- but we weren't. And apparently, again, there just-- they-- we-- there just were too many of us. And the-- and the gas chambers just could not accommodate everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us into the camp, in behind the barbed wires. And we were in barracks. There was one plank of bunks, like one long plank. And you, just like sardines, were stretched out on those planks that was-- and we would be taken to-- daily to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the labor was you dug up sod from this place, and you transport-- moved it over there. You carried it from this place back to that. And it was just forced labor, just meaningless labor, just very hard, meaningless labor. There were periodic-- every time they needed to, to feed the gas chambers, the crematoria, they would come and have a selection. There were periodic selections. And once, my sister was pointed out to be taken away. And my mother said, but that's my child! And I don't know. The German decided to let her stay. So you know, it's just very, very random luck. Now, we were completely committed. We had an agreement that we will not let-- if one of us-- if one of us dies, all of us go. And what happened was once, my sister-- my sister and I went on one of those labor trips, on the work trips. And there was a big ditch. And in that ditch, there were remains of the people who were gassed-- teeth, hair, coins, pictures, all kinds of little personal belongings. And we were ordered-- there was one woman an SS woman, Brygida. I'm sure-- I know that her name is known. And she was very, very cruel. And, and she was guarding us. And we were sorting these items. And there was another girl next to my sister, a young woman, who, who picked up a coin, and she threw it. Now, the German, Brygida, noticed it. And-- but she didn't notice quite who touched it, the coin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took that other young woman and my sister. My-- I was not quite 13. I was 12 years old. She was a year and a half older. So she still was very, very young, too. And she beat my sister to a pulp. She just chopped her up. Well, in a way, that may have even saved our lives because what happened was that we returned-- please, try-- remind me to tell you about a little boy and his mother, OK? I don't want to-- I, I, I want to mention him and his mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and then, when we went back to the barracks-- we were known for being a little bit noisy, my sister and my mother and I. Because my mother always-- if she had that little soup, she would quibble with us. She would want to give us another spoonful of soup. And we said, no, no, no. So they called us monkeys. And, and there, there was one-- I think they were Polish women, non-Jewish. It was one barrack. And the-- and the kapo is the supervisor of the barrack-- was a Polish woman. And she was very sadistic. She was very mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she banished us to a punishment barrack because-- and I also remember that once, there was-- she wanted it to be totally, totally quiet in, in the barrack. And I don't think we were very obedient, my sister and I. We were-- but apparently, she thought that there was a little bit too much noise.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she took out one young woman as a hostage. And she said, if I hear a whisper, I'll beat her to death. And we knew she would. And so of course, there was silence. But anyway, we were banished to this punishment barrack, as though this one wasn't enough. For some reason-- and I don't know if she took a liking to us, but on some level, she, I think, liked us. And that, too, is kind of annoying to me because so often, if somebody would show a glance of humanity, we'd say, oh, they like us. Oh, they are not really so bad. But, you know, bad people have their good moments, too. So maybe we just caught her at a good moment. Well, she kind of-- my sister didn't go to work. She needed, really, to recover a little. And there were no selections during that, that small period of recovery. And my sister remained in camp. And my-- and I-- and my mother and I went to work. While my mother and I were gone, my sister-- a, a, a, a man from Skarzysko, from a labor camp, a German, came over. And he told them that-- and he, he announced that he needs some people to work at HASAG, a, a ammunition camp in Skarzysko. My sister said he sounded [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] believable-- also because she wanted to believe. Anyway, so her name-- she-- her name was put on it. But my mother and I were gone. Now, she, she didn't know-- but everybody believed 'you don't believe it anyway.' They are taking the people to be gassed. So people wanted to trade places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my mother and I returned, we said, you know, we won't-- we won't let her go to the gas chambers alone. So that was-- that was not-- that was not even a choice. That was not even a question. It was something that just-- we were-- it was a very easy decision. And so, so we went to-- and, and then the other women wanted to save themselves. So we-- my sister went to that couple. And she said, look, these people want to trade. You know that we are going to be gassed anyway. What do you care?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she did let-- put both our names on. Well, it happened to have been the truth. And we were sent to the labor camp. And these two women who volunteered are not easy to think of because they were gassed. And to the best of my knowledge, there was only one other group prior to us, a very small group, that came out alive of Skarzysko-- I mean, of Majdanek-- and my mother, my sister, and I, in that-- in that small group. And for three members of a family, three women, to survive, I don't-- it was really a rarity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before I let you leave the camp, I, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --want you to give me a little bit more detail. You mentioned that you slept on planks. But can you  tell me what the barrack was like, what the food was like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what the sanitation was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could give me an idea of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the camp life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to return, for a moment, when we were still out in the field, in front of the barbed wires. That's that little boy and his mother, Piotrusz. There was a neighbor of ours, a beautiful woman, with a little boy who was maybe three years old. And he was-- oh, he was the love of everyone because there were so few left. And he was so gorgeous, with dark curls and, and, and glowing eyes. And, and, and there was such-- in spite-- in spite of all this-- well, he didn't know anything other. His mother was very protective. She, she also had cyanide. Because most of us, that was something that, as far as I know, most people armed themselves with, when-- at, at the very end because we were-- we knew, you know, after the uprising, that's the end. She and her-- and Piotrusz swallowed, swallowed the cyanide. She threw it up. And he died in her arms. [CRYING] She went crazy. She gave herself up. She pleaded. She ran. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the camps like? No bathrooms, barrels outside our, our, our barracks. They humiliated us in every way they could. You held back until you couldn't because you did have to go and use that bathrooms in the open, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the barbed wires, we knew were electrified. And, and there were posts, towers, with, with guards. And if they felt like shooting at you when you went to the bathroom, and if-- you know, it, it, it-- everything you did-- we, we always went to the bathroom together, hoping that if they shoot, that they'll shoot all three of us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- every morning, there was an Appell. Every morning, we were counted. We had to stand at attention. They wanted to teach us a lesson, as though they didn't teach us enough. Somebody was hung. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- it, it was not unusual to see the blue faces, the bodies rigid, dangling from the [NON-ENGLISH]. That, that, that, that was-- that was something that we saw every day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The barrack, the, the-- it was just an open field, sort of a bare field. I remember glancing often beyond the barbed wire. You know, it was-- it was as though hoping to see something normal from beyond the, the, the, the wires, maybe even a possibility of escaping. But I remember that outside was also so forbidding because it-- we were-- it, it, it was so isolated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were just so, so, like, in a no-man's-land. The, the way I remember the long barracks, with pitched roofs, only one, one level, and they were lined up side by side. There was a distance between them and even rows on either side. Inside the, the barracks, you walked in on wood planks. And you stepped in through that wooden door, and there-- just a, a small distance, maybe three, four feet, maybe, maybe two feet. I would say maybe a distance like this. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you had the row of the barracks-- of the-- of the-- what do you call the, the planks on which we slept? The bunks?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bunks. And the bunks ran the length of the-- of the barrack. And it was-- and it was two or three tiers. I think probably three. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The clothes that we had was when-- excuse me. When we came out of the showers, there were piles of clothes. And, and I remember that when we went into the showers, and I had to give up my clothes, I had my father's picture [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in my shoe under that layer that is, you know, the top layer inside the shoe. And I said, oh, could I just at least keep that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he whipped me. And of course, he left and took it away. But that was-- that was before, before we entered in the barracks-- I mean, before we entered the, the, the camp. The clothes that we had, that we wore was that we-- when we went out of the showers, we picked whatever was there. And, you know, if you-- you did it in a hurry. If you found two shoes that matched, fine. If not, that was too bad. We were very scantily, very-- just, just very little clothes. The food, we had soup, a lot of-- a lot of turnip soup. And my friends know that if they make vegetable soup, they don't put in turnips because they know that I-- the, the, the scent of turnips is just very, very repelling to me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The, the rations were very, very meager. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, I guess that pretty much describes it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had you had your cyanide hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sewn in, in-- inside a lapel of, of, of my coat or jacket-- coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you took the shower--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that was the end of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was the end of it because we did not have anything. We-- at this point, everything that ever belonged to us was separated from us. We did not-- later on, I'll show you a few pictures that I have of my father and a couple of cousins. That, too, was given to us by relatives who lived here. But we had nothing, not a thing that, that, that belonged to us prior to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You told me that you were transported out of Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Well, the next thing we knew, when we were put in the-- we were put into, again, freight trains. And that, you know-- the, the random luck that accounted for our survival. It was-- one thing was, was that we had one another, which usually worked against survival. In our case, it, it worked to accommodate our survival. Another thing was absolute, random luck. They loaded us on trains. And apparently, they took some men, too. And, and the, the train was-- I don't know how many cars, but there were several. At some point-- the, the distance from, from Majdanek to Skarzysko, I don't know how far it is. But, but it was quite a distance. At some point, the train stopped. And they told us to go out in the field if we have to use the bathroom. Well, my sister had to go to the bathroom. And we were saying, oh, Frieda, try not to go. Because if we go out, they'll probably shoot us. She says, but I have to go. So she went out. And then they rushed us back into the trains. And we got on, on another train. Well, as it worked out, when we got into, into Skarzysko, there were three camps. There was [NON-ENGLISH] A, B, and C. [NON-ENGLISH], which is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'camp.' I, I can't think of the translation right now. But they were three different camps, were three-- they, all three of them, were labor camps and ammunition factories. But in one, I think it was [NON-ENGLISH] C-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] A, B, and C. The [NON-ENGLISH] C was producing only the gunpowder. Before the war, when you worked there, you could-- you only worked a, a couple of hours maybe a day, and only at a very short periods of time because the gunpowder discolored your skin. And it also was toxic for your lungs. It, it, it, it settled on your lungs. That was-- that was like a death camp because they worked the Jews there until they expired.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if we had stayed in this first car, if my sister would not have jumped out of the train, and, and, and if we would not have gone with her, she would not have had to use the field-- go to the bathroom, we would have definitely gone there. And this would have been the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I don't remember what the other camp was. Our camp was the best one, in the sense that survival was the most possible for the duration. In, in other words, you did not die off as quickly. And there, the barracks look pretty much the same as the-- as the barracks in, in Majdanek, the same field, and pretty much the same-- the same-- organized pretty much the same way. Outside, also barbed wires. And outside the camp, there was a forest. And I remember the forest. It invited me as much as it frightened me. Because one of the-- one of the camp-- the person who is in charge in camp-- of the camp, he was a short man. And he was a hunchback and a sadist. He would come in periodically for the sport. And he would take people out and just shoot them in, in, in, in the-- in the forest. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went-- from there, we went to work every morning. I-- we, we worked on a night shift. So-- and maybe, maybe switched off. I don't recall. But I recall much more working night shifts. We worked together. We, we slept together. And, and, and we were together. Then again, another, another coincidence that was pretty, pretty awful saved my sister's life and saved all of our lives. My sister-- one day-- that was in the winter. One day-- we, the-- we had latrines that were a little distant-- were in, in the center of-- the latrines were enclosed latrines, holes in the ground in Skarzysko this time. And, and they were located in the center of the camp, which meant that you had to leave your barracks if you had to go to use the latrine. But you also were not supposed to be seen. So, you know, no matter what you did was not-- did not work. But-- so we all had [NON-ENGLISH]. And it's a-- it's a-- it's an empty can. And we used that [NON-ENGLISH], that empty can, to, to, to urinate. And especially, we, we used it during the winter. And that happened during the winter. Because outside, everything was covered with ice. It was slippery. And at that time, what we were wearing were just, just one layer of clothing-- winter and very, very cold. My sister, my mother, and I had-- our, our legs were completely covered with oozing scabs. We had scabies. We had the mange. We were just so disgusting. My mother always used to say, my children, my children were so beautiful. And people would never-- and she would always say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They were so beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should we pause? Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
